canonical_or_religious_significance: One of most sacred sites of Śvetāmbara tradition within Jainism; along with Shikharji believed to be among holiest pilgrimage places
historical_significance: Large complex of Jain temples located on Shatrunjaya hills near Palitana in Bhavnagar district, Gujarat. Dense collection of almost 900 small shrines and large temples led many to call Palitana 'city of temples'. Earliest temples in complex date as far back as 11th century CE. There are altogether 863 Palitana Jain temples made up of marble. Main temple dedicated to first Tirthankara Rishabhanath. Construction of temples started in 11th century believed to have taken around 900 years to complete. In 14th and 15th century these Jain temples destroyed during Muslim invasion. Temples believed to have been renovated about sixteen times since then. First temples in this location erected by Kumarpal Solanki, famous Jain devotee. Temples demolished by Turkish Muslim invaders in 1311 AD but eventually reconstructed to their splendour. Main temple situated at height that can be reached by traversing about 3500 steps from foot hills. In Jain tradition, believed that 23 of 24 Tirthankaras, all except Neminatha, sanctified Palitana with visits.

Located within three kilometres of Mount Abu in Rajasthan. Beautiful example of Jain architecture. Exterior of temples offers simple outlook but aesthetic artwork of interior reflects marvellous architecture. Temples existing beyond inception of town tell tales of bygone Jain architecture of Rajasthan. Dedicated to Adinath, first Tirthankara. Temples assembled between 11th and 13th centuries by Chalukya Dynasty. Now handled by Seth Shri Kalyanji Anandji Pedhi, Sirohi. Temples imitate Maru-Gurjara architecture. Temple carved from pristine white marble exhibits intricate engraved craftsmanship. Edifices took around 14 years to complete construction employed around 1500 masons and 1200 labourers. Expense for edifices went up to ₹185.3 million. Temples isolated within forest above 4000 feet in height consist of five temples. Engraved patterns include arabesque patterns, lotus buds, petals, other floral designs and figurines of gods and goddesses in various dancing postures.

canonical_or_religious_significance: One of largest and among most important temples in Jain culture in India
historical_significance: One of highlights of Rajasthan situated between Udaipur and Jodhpur. Temple bears testimony to cultural heritage of Indian architecture. Celebrates Adinath, first Tirthankar of Avasarpiṇi according to Jain cosmology. 15th-century Jain businessman Darna Shah, Porwal from Ghanerao began construction after being enlightened by celestial vision. Patronage of temple was Rana Kumbha, then monarch of Mewar. Built in 15th century by Jain businessman Seth Dharna with help of Maharaja Rana Kumbha. Took 50 years to complete this magnificent structure. Milk-white marble temple stretches over 48,000 square feet comprises 5 Shikharas, 2 pillar halls and 400 columns supporting 80 domes, thus pavilion has total of 1444 individually intrinsic engraved pillars. Temple consists of 84 bhonyra or underground chambers built to protect Jain idols from Mughals. Has distinctive domes, cupolas, turrets and shikhara.

canonical_or_religious_significance: According to Jain tradition, Lord Neminath, 22nd Tirthankara in Jainism, attained moksha on Girnar Mountain
historical_significance: Sacred marvels atop Gujarat's Girnar mountain showcasing exquisite Jain architecture and spiritual tranquility. Roots of Girnar Jain Temples date back to ancient times making them integral part of India's religious heritage. Sacred connection elevates temples to revered status among Jain community. Historical timeline intricate with construction and renovations spanning various dynasties including Mauryas, Guptas and Solankis. Neminath Temple dedicated to 22nd Tirthankara Lord Neminath one of oldest and most revered temples on Mount Girnar. Believed to have been built in 12th century. Pilgrims climb 3,666 steps to seek blessings at this sacred site. Mallinath Temple dating back to 12th century dedicated to 19th Tirthankara Mallinath. Temple masterpiece of architecture featuring exquisite carvings depicting scenes from Jain mythology.

Nestled in between tranquility of Aravalli Hills in Gujarat. One of oldest and most beautiful Jain temples in Gujarat. Dedicated to Lord Adinatha commonly known as Rishabdev, first tirthankara of Jain community. Stands as magnificent testament to architectural excellence of Solanki Dynasty. Built in 12th century by Solanki dynasty king Kumarapala under watch of Acharya Hemachandra. Follows Svetambara Jain Tradition. Considered as one of those jain holy sites that have blended artistic excellence and devotion greatly. Pilgrimage center near Kheralu in Mehsana district with two compounds of Jain temples that are important examples of Māru-Gurjara style of architecture. Ajitnatha temple constructed in 1161 by Solanki king Kumarapala under advice of his teacher Acharya Hemachandra. Both main sects of Jainism represented with adjoining walled compounds: Śvetāmbara compound consists of 14 temples in all and five Digambara-affiliated temples.

Part of UNESCO World Heritage Site of Ellora Caves in Maharashtra. Over 100 caves at site all excavated from basalt cliffs in Charanandri Hills, 34 of which open to public. These consist of 17 Hindu, 12 Buddhist and 5 Jain caves. Five caves actually 23 distinct excavations over different periods. Jain caves at Ellora likely began in late 8th century with construction and excavation activity extending beyond 10th century and into 13th century before coming to halt with invasion of region by Delhi Sultanate. Excavated in 9th century CE signify last major phase of religious and artistic activity at Ellora celebrating ascetic character of Jaina sect. Particularly important Jain shrines are Chhota Kailash (cave 30), Indra Sabha (cave 32) and Jagannath Sabha (cave 33). Cave 32 or Indra Sabha double storied cave with monolithic sarvatobhadra shrine, manastambha or pillar and free-standing elephant in court. Largest and most elaborately decorated cave in group with beautiful pillars, large sculptural panels and paintings on ceiling.

Located in Mundra Taluka of Kutch District, Gujarat. Has vast historical importance and popular tourist attraction. According to Bantvijaya Chronicles, 19th-century Jain scripture, temples date back to 516 BCE during reign of Raja Siddhasen, though there is no historical evidence to support this claim. First reference to temple dates back to 8th century. Temple believed by tradition to have been established by Jain layman named Devchandra centuries ago. Magnificently sculpted with intricate and striking carvings. Temple renovated and rehabilitated from time to time as damaged many times due to earthquake. Temple extensively renovated by Jagdusha in 13th century. Since then temple has been renovated nine times. Constructed predominantly in white marble. Temple complex features majestic pillars, ornate carvings and elaborately adorned shrines. Central shrine houses three exquisite marble images: Ajitnatha, Shantinatha and Parshvanatha each representing profound spiritual ideals. Surrounding main shrine are 52 smaller dev-kulikas adding to temple's architectural splendor.

Major Śvetāmbara Jain tirth (pilgrimage site) of Tirthankara Parshwanatha in Rajasthan located between villages of Vikrampura and Nakoda in Barmer district. Temple dedicated to Jain tirthankar Lord Parashvanath but temple more popular for Nakoda Bheru ji who is custodian god of this temple. According to Jain legend, temple initially built by Jain acharya Sthulabhadra in 3rd century BCE. However, current structure constructed in 11th century CE by Solanki Dynasty. Temple has about 246 inscriptions which amply show that over centuries temple was extended, renovated, rebuilt and additions made to it. Even today construction work continuing. In year 1155 VS (1098 CE) Sajjan Shah re-built/renovated Shankheshwar Parshwanath Jain Temple. In fourteenth century VS temple destroyed by Alauddin Khalji. In sixteenth century VS under inspiration of Vijaysensuri new temple with 52 idols built.
approx_established_period: 11th century CE, legendary origins 3rd century BCE

historical_significance: Situated in Mahudi town in Mansa taluka of Gandhinagar district, Gujarat. Pilgrimage centre of Jains and other communities visiting temple of Jain deity Ghantakarna Mahavir and Padmaprabhu Jain Temple. Known as Madhupuri historically. Derasar considered to be one of most sacred Jain pilgrimages situated in Mahudi, Mehsana District, spreads over area of 2 square kilometers. In ancient times place known as Madhumati. Idols and artistic remains recovered from ground show history of this place nearly 2000 years old based on inscriptions in Brahmi script.

Historical temple complex in town of Songadh in Bhavnagar district, Saurashtra region in Gujarat. 8 km from Sihor town and 28 km from Bhavnagar town. Temple complex consist of eight temples. Svadhyaya Mandir or study temple first temple in temple complex built in 1937. Temple white marble structure with teachings of Kundakund engraved on walls and embossed on golden leaf. Jinendra Dharmasabha houses samavasarana, divine preaching hall of Tirthankaras, of Simandhara based on description by Mahavideha. Temple has murals with depiction of knowledge of true doctrine cascading like water steam from Simandhara to Kundakunda and Kundakunda to Kanji Swami.

Located in center of Shankheshwar town of Patan district, Gujarat. Temple dedicated to Parshwanath important place of pilgrimage for followers of Jainism. Oldest, historical and major temple of Jainism after Shatrunjaya hill in Palitana. In ancient scriptures this Tirtha referred to as Shankhapur. In year 1155 VS (1098 CE) Sajjan Shah re-built/renovated Shankheshwar Parshwanath Jain Temple on banks of Rupen river. In Vikram Samvat 1286 (1229 CE) Vastupala-Tejpal renovated this temple under instructions of Vardhamansuri. There were 52 idols in temple. In fourteenth century VS temple destroyed by Alauddin Khalji. In sixteenth century VS under inspiration of Vijaysensuri new temple with 52 idols built. In VS 1760 (1703 CE) sangha built new temple and got idol reinstalled. Current temple built in 1811. Masterpiece of Jain architecture featuring intricate carvings, ornate pillars and beautifully designed domes.

Located in Ahmedabad, Gujarat. Quiet island of peace amid noise and activity. Part of Ahmedabad's rich heritage rooted in commerce. Built in fine white marble typical representative of Jain style of architecture. Built under supervision of architect Premchand Salat. Famous saint Shanti Sagar Suri did consecration called Pratishtha. Believed around 400,000 pilgrims attended ceremonies. Event marked with 21 days celebration. Highly ornate and enduring architectural structure accommodates 238 stone images and 21 yantras. Main structure in white marble two-storeyed houses idol of Mulnayak Lord Dharamnath, 15th Tirthankar. Sacred complex topped off with huge twelve rings semi circular dome standing with support of eight pillars. Dome 80 feet in diameter stands at height of 350 meters. Impressive idol of different Tirthankaras set in 52 shrines called devikulas located along circumambulatory passage.

Jain temple in Lodhruva village of Jaisalmer district in Rajasthan. Belongs to Śvetāmbara sect of Jainism. Ancient and historically significant temple holds immense religious importance. Believed to have been built in 10th century during reign of Parmar ruler Bhati Rao dedicated to Jain Tirthankara Parsvanatha. Showcases exquisite blend of intricate carvings, detailed sculptures and remarkable architecture typical of medieval times. Built with yellow limestone and sandstone famous for intricate craftsmanship. Temple features ornate torana (arched gateway) and stone carvings of Kalpavriksha and kalputra. Considered one of best example of Jain architecture in Rajasthan. Temple walls folded similar to accordion features exquisite screen carvings of jali. Temple's facade features finely sculpted figures of gods and goddesses, celestial nymphs and intricate floral patterns carved with great skill and precision.

In back streets of Osian near Jodhpur worth visiting whilst in desert state of Rajasthan. Temple amongst oldest Jain temple dating to 9th century. Important pilgrimage of Oswal Jain community. In center of temple lies gigantic 32 inch sand and cow milk statue of Lord Mahavira in padmasana posture. Great carvings and beautiful painted story board of life of last Tirthankara. Beautiful sand stone carvings. Level of peace and tranquility maintained here unmatchable. Built in soft red sand-stone has stunning intricate patterns.

One and half km away from Ambaji town in Banaskantha district, Gujarat. Has historical jain temple of Shri Neminath Bhagvan which dates back to 13th century. Kumbhariya jain temple of Neminath Bhagwan now heritage center in Gujarat. Beautiful carving on wall main attraction of temple. From remains of group of 360 Temples built by Vimalsha in year 1032. Five separate temples honouring Mahavir, Sambhavnath, Neminath, Parshwanath and Shantinath. Vimal Shah, minister to Chalukyan monarch Bhimdev I, constructed 360 Jain temples out of white marble but today only five remain in original form. Temple has facility of dharamshala and also bhojanshala for devotees.

canonical_or_religious_significance: Ancient Siddha and Atishaya Kshetra; in Jain tradition, believed about 3.5 crore munis attained salvation from this place
historical_significance: Ancient Siddha and Atishaya Kshetra located on border of Madhya Pradesh and Maharashtra. Comes under Bhainsdehi tehsil of Betul district. 52 temples on mountain surrounding beautiful waterfall. First temple of lord Parshwanathji model of architecture. Idol ancient and glorified with seven snake hoods. In devotional tradition, believed sandal-saffron shower obtained on every Ashtami Chaudas and poornima. Most temples built in 16th century and later on. Temple no. 10 famous as Mendhagiri which carved in mountain itself very ancient one built in 10th century. Design and structure of pillars, roof worth to watch. Main temples are 1st Temple, 10th Temple, 26th Temple and 40th Temple.

alternative_names: Shri Digamber Jain Siddha Kshetra Kundalpur
canonical_or_religious_significance: In Jain tradition, Moksha Sthana - place of salvation for Shridhar Kevali
historical_significance: Important pilgrimage site for Jains in Damoh district of Madhya Pradesh. Lies 37 kilometers northeast of Damoh city. Home to numerous Jain temples including renowned Bade Baba Temple dedicated to Rishabhanatha (also called Bade Baba or Adinath). Bade Baba Statue most prominent feature standing atop hill in striking 15-foot form of Rishabhanatha seated in padmasana on 3-foot pedestal. Central figure flanked by two statues of Parshvanatha of equal height. Bade Baba Temple most renowned of Kundalpur's 64 temples. Originally established during 8th century CE houses ancient idol dating back to post-Gupta period. Temple complex rediscovered by Bhattaraka Surendrakirti of Mulasangha-Balatkaragana-Sarasvati Gachchha lineage who with support from Bundela ruler Chhatrasal led its reconstruction. Starting in 1999 construction began on new larger structure incorporating elements of Nagara and modern Rajasthani architectural styles with aim of making it one of tallest Jain temples in world. Spread across 1000 acres.
approx_established_period: 8th century CE, renovated 1700 CE, new temple 1999

historical_significance: Important Jain temple in Jaipur Rajasthan. Dedicated to Shantinatha 16th Tirthankara. Important pilgrimage site for Jains in Rajasthan. Features traditional Rajasthani architecture.

Main temple at Ranakpur complex. Part of larger Ranakpur temple complex. Features Chaumukha (four-faced) idol of Adinatha facing four directions. Garbhagriha or sanctum sanctorum houses principal Chaumukha Adinatha idol. Important component of Ranakpur Jain temple architecture.

Part of Dilwara temple complex at Mount Abu. Built by Vimala Shah, minister of Solanki king Bhimdev I, in 1031 CE. Oldest of five Dilwara temples. Dedicated to first Tirthankara Rishabhanatha. Considered masterpiece of architecture featuring exquisite marble carvings. Main hall has 48 pillars all beautifully carved. Features intricate ceiling work.

Part of Dilwara temple complex at Mount Abu. Built by Vastupala and Tejapala, ministers of Solanki ruler Viradhavala, in 1230 CE. Dedicated to 22nd Tirthankara Neminatha. Features incredibly detailed marble carvings. Central dome considered architectural marvel with intricate lotus carving at center from which 16 brackets emerge supporting ring of 72 figures of goddesses. Important example of later Solanki period architecture.
